local_names.romanized_local: Lor Mee Gai (Cantonese); ကော်ရည်ခေါက်ဆွဲ လျာဂျွမ်းပြန် (Burmese)

region_of_origin: Singapore (Hokkien-Teochew origin); also served in Myanmar-style variant

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Lor Mee. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Lor Mee entry. A0FHR (noodle dish with meat) is the closest defensible parent in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Lor Mee or equivalent braised noodle dish with chicken.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Lor Mee with Chicken weighs approximately 400 g and contains thick yellow noodles in a starchy braised gravy topped with sliced chicken, a hard-boiled egg, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Lor Mee (Noodles in Thick Gravy) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Lor Mee (noodles in thick gravy) as a composite dish. The chicken variant is nutritionally comparable to the standard Lor Mee entry. Values are adapted from HPB Lor Mee per 100 g cooked weight. Protein is slightly adjusted upward to reflect the chicken topping replacing the typical pork/seafood mix, based on USDA SR Legacy chicken breast cooked values as a secondary reference for the protein increment. Energy, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, and fibre values are anchored to the HPB Lor Mee entry.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat-based, cooked — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate for thick braised noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lor Mee with Chicken. GI of 50 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat-based noodles (GI range 40–55, medium-low). The thick starchy gravy (cornstarch/tapioca-thickened) may modestly elevate GI relative to plain noodles; a midpoint estimate of 50 is used. GL per serving = (50 × 63.2 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 31.6. Classified as low GI per the <55 threshold.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1920 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Lor Mee with Chicken to occasional consumption and request reduced-sodium gravy, as a single bowl can provide approximately 80–96% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~31.6 per bowl) from thick wheat noodles and starchy braised gravy
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Lor Mee with Chicken in reduced portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glucose response, given the moderate-to-high glycemic load per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from braised gravy and chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Lor Mee with Chicken should be consumed infrequently; choosing skinless chicken and requesting less gravy can reduce sodium and saturated fat intake to more cardioprotective levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lor Mee with Chicken (卤面鸡肉; Lor Mee Ayam) is a Singaporean braised noodle dish of Hokkien-Teochew origin, served at hawker centres and Chinese coffeeshops across Singapore. The dish consists of thick, flat yellow wheat noodles submerged in a dark, viscous, starch-thickened braised gravy made from dark soy sauce, five-spice powder, garlic, and vinegar. The chicken variant replaces the traditional pork belly and seafood toppings with sliced braised or poached chicken, typically accompanied by a hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, and a dollop of sambal chilli and vinegar on the side. The gravy has a savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly tangy flavour profile with a gelatinous, coating texture. This version is sold at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant in Singapore, reflecting a Burmese-influenced preparation style (ကော်ရည်ခေါက်ဆွဲ လျာဂျွမ်းပြန်) that retains the core Singaporean Lor Mee character. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g bowl provides approximately 472 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 12.4 g fat, and 63.2 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1920 mg per bowl), making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic load is moderate (~31.6 per bowl), requiring portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or soy.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Lor Mee with Chicken specifically. Values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Lor Mee (standard) entry with a protein adjustment for the chicken topping. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation and estimation steps.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Lor Mee (standard) entry per 100 g cooked weight, with protein adjusted upward to reflect chicken topping (reference: USDA SR Legacy, chicken breast cooked, item 05064). 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 8. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 9. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 10. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. 12. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/zero for this dish type per HPB source. 13. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked wheat noodles); no direct Lor Mee GI measurement found in peer-reviewed literature. 14. Halal status is uncertain as traditional Lor Mee may contain pork-derived ingredients; the chicken variant at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant may be halal-prepared but this is unconfirmed. 15. The Burmese name ကော်ရည်ခေါက်ဆွဲ လျာဂျွမ်းပြန် is preserved from the input record as the Myanmar-language descriptor for this dish style. 16. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is primarily a Singaporean hawker food. 17. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002473 and FoodEx2 code A0FHR are closest-parent matches only; no exact ontology code exists for Lor Mee.
